title: BETWEEN WESLEY ROSTRON v DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL IN THE MATTER BETWEEN WESLEY ROSTRON CLAIMANT AND SEA LEVEL ECO DISTILLERY LIMITED
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT
AND DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 IN THE
MATTER BETWEEN 

 WESLEY
 ROSTRON 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 SEA
 LEVEL ECO DISTILLERY LIMITED 

 RESPONDENT 

 TRIBUNAL
 JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025]
TRE 33 

 Date of Hearing: 24 September 2025 

 Before: Mrs
Hannah Westmacott, Deputy Chairman 

 For the Claimant: In
person 

 For
the Respondent: Ms
Sarah Gaudion 

 DECISION 

 The Claimant’s claim for breach of contract succeeds
and the Respondent shall pay £1,308 to the Claimant by way of damages. The
Claimant is responsible for paying any tax or social security contributions due
on the damages awarded. 

REASONS 

 Introduction 

 1. 
 The Claimant was employed by the Respondent to work as
a Distillery Manager from 7 to 18 January 2025, when he was dismissed with
immediate effect and without payment in lieu of notice. 

 2. 
 On 3 March 2025, the Claimant presented a claim against
the Respondent for breach of contract for failure to give notice of termination
of his employment. The Respondent defends the claim on the grounds that it was
entitled to dismiss the Claimant without notice because he committed gross
misconduct, in that he failed to properly lock the Respondent’s premises when
he left on 17 January 2025. 

 3. 
 A case management meeting was held on the claim at
which a final hearing (the “Hearing”) was listed. In advance of the Hearing,
witness statements were provided to the Tribunal from the Claimant and, on
behalf of the Respondent, Sarah Gaudion. Both parties also provided a small
bundle of documents in addition to their witness statements. Both witnesses
attended the Hearing, where they were questioned on the evidence before the
Tribunal. 

 Evidence 

 4. The Claimant
worked for approximately 6 to 7 days during his employment with the Respondent,
one day was spent at the Respondent’s distillery and the remainder at the
Respondent’s shop (the “Shop”). Ms Gaudion asserts the Claimant was responsible
for locking the Shop every day that he worked there. The Claimant said he
locked up a few times, but that once or twice he left with or before someone
else, who locked the Shop. 

 5. On 17
January 2025, Ms Gaudion and the Claimant were both present in the Shop and
they held a discussion, during which they agreed that the Claimant’s employment
would terminate and the Claimant would work and be paid for his 2 weeks’ notice
period. At the end of his shift, after Ms Gaudion left, the Claimant closed the
Shop. 

 6. There were 2
locks on the Shop door. At the Hearing, the Claimant could not recall the exact
position of the locks; he had not operated them for over 8 months and even then
he only did so a few times. Ms Gaudion, having had the benefit of still using
the locks on a daily basis, explained that one lock was right at the top of the
door and the other was at the bottom, near to the ground. She stated that the
locks were new in, or shortly before, January 2025 and both operated well and
easily. 

 7. The Claimant
was certain he locked both locks when he closed the Shop on 17 January 2025. At
the Hearing, he explained that he was very safety conscious and he made sure he
properly locked the door, checking the handle and looking through the window to
check that everything was switched off inside the Shop. He recalled that even
after he started walking to his car he turned back to check the handle again. He
said that he was particularly careful because locking the Shop was new to him. He
had been a key holder in many of his prior roles and had always locked up properly. 

 8. However, Ms
Gaudion asserts that, when she went to open the Shop on 18 January 2025, she bent
down to open the bottom lock then, as she stood up, she put her hand on the
handle and the door opened, without her having to undo the top lock.
Consequently, she asserts the top lock was not locked by the Claimant the day
before. 

 9. Ms Gaudion said
she had planned to speak to the Claimant about this when he arrived at work at
11am, but he did not turn up at this time. Ms Gaudion, therefore, called the
Claimant at 11.11am, who said he did not realise his shift started at 11am,
thinking it started at midday as it had the previous day. Ms Gaudion told the
Claimant not to come in. The call lasted 28 seconds and Ms Gaudion did not
mention the door not being properly locked on this call. 

 10. Ms Gaudion
then emailed the Claimant at 3.20pm stating: 

 “Following
a no show for your shift today, on top of that the fact that the shop door was
not locked last night, your employment has been terminated with immediate
effect.” 

 11. 
 The Claimant responded to this email at 5.43pm stating: 

 “I can
confirm that I always ensure the door is properly locked before leaving. If
there was an issue with the door being left unlocked, I am unsure how that may
have occurred.” and “…I had checked it 3 times
before leaving...” 

 12. 
 Ms Gaudion wrote to the Claimant again on 20 January
2025 at 11.01am stating: 

 “I know
the door wasn’t locked as I opened up on Saturday morning. The bottom lock had
been locked but not the top lock…” 

 13. 
 Again, the
Claimant responded the same day at 10.06pm stating: 

 “I am
still confident that I securely locked the door properly on the Friday night
(both locks).” 

 14. The
Respondent has provided a copy of the Disciplinary Procedure pages of its handbook,
which gives examples of gross misconduct and specifies the following as one: 

 “Failing
to secure the property during closing hours or any action that invalidates our
insurance.” 

 15. 
 The handbook also goes on to state that: 

 “Gross
Misconduct may lead to immediate suspension or dismissal without prior warning,
depending on the severity of the offense.” 

 16. The handbook
is not referred to in the contract of employment between the parties. At the
hearing, Ms Gaudion said that she did not provide the Claimant with a copy of
the handbook, but that it was on the Respondent’s electronic document system,
which the Claimant had access to. She noted that one of the tasks that she had
discussed with the Claimant during a meeting on 7 January 2025 was that, as
distillery manager with responsibility for other staff, he should review the
staff handbook to check that it was appropriate. She provided a copy of a page
from a notebook that she left in the Shop for the Claimant, on which she had
written “ Review handbook Lizzie done ”. She asserted she wrote this
following their meeting on 7 January 2025 and that the Claimant could have
looked for the handbook himself on the document system or asked where to find
it. 

 17. The Claimant
said he was never provided with a copy of the handbook. He said he was aware of
the electronic document system and that he could access it on the Respondent’s
computer, but he was not or told where to find any particular documents on the
system. 

 Conclusion 

 18. The Claimant
does not have sufficient length of service to make a claim for unfair
dismissal. Accordingly, the tribunal does not need to make a determination as
to the fairness of the dismissal. The relevant question is not what was in the
mind of the person dismissing the Claimant, as it would be when determining
whether a dismissal is fair. Instead, the tribunal must determine whether the
Claimant actually committed gross misconduct, sufficient to entitle the
Respondent to dismiss him without notice. 

 Was the Door Locked? 

 19. The Respondent
asserts the gross misconduct was the Claimant’s failure to properly lock the
Shop door. I, therefore, first considered whether the Claimant properly locked the
Shop door. 

 20. Within a few
days of the Claimant closing the Shop and Ms Gaudion opening it, they both sent
emails, respectively, saying that the Claimant locked both locks and that Ms
Gaudion found the top lock unlocked the next day. Accordingly, I do not find
that either witness is misremembering whether the top lock was locked or not. Upon
considering the oral evidence at the Hearing, I also do not believe that either
party was intentionally seeking to deceive me, and I cannot say that I believed
one witness more than the other on this point. 

 21. Accordingly,
I considered whether either the Claimant or Ms Gaudion could have been
mistaken. Given Ms Gaudion’s explanation that she did not even have to put her
key into the top lock before reaslising it was not locked, I think it is
unlikely she was mistaken. It is possible that the Claimant put his key in the
top lock and removed it again, thinking it was locked, but without actually
locking it. He said he checked the handle after locking up, but Ms Gaudion gave
evidence that the handle would not open the door even if only one lock was
locked. However, Ms Gaudion also gave evidence that the lock was new and easy
to operate, so it is also unlikely that the Claimant made a mistake. 

 22. Consequently,
I considered whether there were any other possibilities, for example, could something
have happened to the top lock in between the Claimant closing the Shop and Ms
Gaudion opening it. No evidence was put forward on this point and it does not
appear to have been something the parties investigated. There may have been
other keyholders and Ms Gaudion gave evidence that, at the time, the alarm and
CCTV systems were not in operation. Accordingly, it is possible the top lock
could have been tampered with or used by someone else in the interim. 

 23. Based on the
evidence before me, I cannot say which possibility is more likely. However, it
is for the Respondent to prove, on a balance of probabilities, that the
Claimant committed the act it asserts constitutes gross misconduct, and I am
not satisfied that the Respondent has done this. Accordingly, the Claimant’s
claim for failure to give notice succeeds on this basis. 

 24. However, in
case I am wrong, and the Claimant mistakenly only locked one of the two locks,
I considered whether this would constitute gross misconduct entitling the
Respondent to dismiss him without notice. 

 What Constitutes Gross Misconduct? 

 25. The Royal
Court, at paragraph 165 of its judgment in the case of Pender v GGH (Jersey) Limited and Ors [2023]JRC124 ,
noted that there is no statutory definition of gross misconduct in either
Jersey or England and Wales. This was followed by a reference to the UK
judgment in the case of Neary v. Dean of Westminster [1999] IRLR
288, which defined gross misconduct as conduct that “ so undermines the
relationship of trust and confidence... that [the employer] should no longer be
required to retain [the employee] in his employment ”. The Royal Court found
that this definition made it clear that what is being considered is exceptionally
serious misconduct such as to merit summary dismissal. 

 26. Following
this, to determine whether conduct constitutes gross misconduct, it is relevant
to consider how damaging the conduct is to the relationship between the
parties. It is uncontroversial to say that dishonesty and other deliberate
actions can damage relationships, but in some cases considerable negligence, which
may consist of a repeated or inexcusable failure to comply with reasonable
instructions, may also be significantly damaging. 

 27. The
Deputy Chairman of the Tribunal at paragraph 4.15 of his judgment in the case
of Jeanette Mash v States Employment Board [2023] TRE 53 
stated that, “in terms of gross misconduct, the Tribunal should consider
“both the character of the conduct and whether it was reasonable for the Trust
[employer] to regard the conduct as having the character of gross misconduct on
the facts” ( Sandwell & West Birmingham Hospitals NHS Trust v
Westwood UKEAT/0032/09).” 

 28. Failing to
lock up a property could be considered gross misconduct in some circumstances,
in particular, if it is done deliberately or recklessly. In this case, there is
no assertion that it was done deliberately and, based on the considerations set
out above, I am not satisfied that the Claimant’s actions were reckless. I am,
therefore, considering only whether, if one of two locks was mistakenly left
unlocked and the door, having been checked, did not open, this would constitute
gross misconduct. 

 29. In my
judgment it is not reasonable to consider that such conduct so undermined the
relationship of trust and confidence between the parties that the Respondent
was entitled to dismiss the Claimant with immediate effect. The conduct was not
repeated and neither was it, in my view, inexcusable. 

 30. I note that
Ms Gaudion also referenced other issues she had with the Claimant’s conduct,
although she does not assert that these constitute gross misconduct. However,
even considering the other conduct issues together with mistakenly failing to lock
one of the two locks, I still do not consider that it is reasonable to regard
the Claimant’s conduct as damaging the relationship between the parties to such
an extent so as to justify summary dismissal. 

 31. Before
finally concluding on this point, I considered the Respondent’s reference to
the examples of gross misconduct set out in its handbook, which includes a failure
to secure the Shop when closed and anything which invalidates the Respondent’s
insurance, which the Respondent asserts only locking one lock would do. 

 32. I note that,
if the contract between the parties lists examples of gross misconduct,
although not necessarily determinative, this may assist in justifying dismissal
without notice on the grounds of the misconduct. 

 33. However, I
do not find that the handbook was incorporated into the contract between the
parties. There is no reference to the handbook in the contract and even on the
Respondent’s evidence, the Claimant was not provided with a finalized copy of
the handbook. While Ms Gaudion asserts she gave the Claimant the task of
checking whether the handbook (which he was to find for himself on the
electronic document system) was appropriate as part of his role in managing
other staff, this is not the same as incorporating the terms of the handbook into
the Claimant’s contract. 

 34. Accordingly,
in the circumstances of this case, I do not find that the conduct of the
Claimant constitutes gross misconduct. The Respondent’s failure to give the
Claimant notice of his dismissal is, therefore, in breach of the contract
between the parties and the Claimant is entitled to damages in respect of any
loss suffered because of that breach. 

 Award 

 35. 
 The parties agree the Claimant was entitled to two
weeks’ notice of dismissal. 

 36. 
 The Claimant has provided several emails and letters
demonstrating that he sought alternative employment during his two weeks’
notice period, and gave evidence that he was not able to secure alternative
employment until after it expired. Accordingly, he has demonstrated that he
sought to mitigate his loss but was unable to so do and is, therefore, awarded
the full two weeks’ pay in lieu of notice. 

 37. The parties
agreed at the Hearing that the Claimant weekly wage was £654, based on an
annual salary of £34,000 divided by 52. Accordingly, had the Claimant worked
for his two weeks’ notice period, he would have been paid £1,308. 

 38. The
Respondent is, therefore, ordered to pay the Claimant damages of £1,308. The Claimant is responsible for paying any tax or
social security contributions due on the damages
awarded. 

 Mrs
H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman Sent to the parties on: 6 October 2025 

 Page Last Updated: 14 Nov 2025
